title: Manufacturing Business Outlook Survey (MBOS) - March 2026 Report
summary: The March 2026 Manufacturing Business Outlook Survey indicates continued expansion in the Philadelphia Fed's manufacturing region, with the general activity index rising from 16.3 to 18.1 for its third consecutive increase. The current shipments index reached 22.2, its highest reading since January 2025, while the employment index turned slightly positive at 0.8. Both price indexes rose notably, with prices paid at 44.7 and prices received at 21.2. Firms expect continued growth over the next six months, with the future employment index jumping 26 points to 40.4, and 82% report uncertainty as at least a slight constraint on capacity utilization.
key_points: General activity diffusion index rose from 16.3 in February to 18.1 in March, the third consecutive increase
Current shipments index jumped 22 points to 22.2, its highest reading since January 2025; 40% of firms reported increases
Current new orders index fell 3 points to 8.6 but remained positive; 54% reported increases
Employment index rose 2 points to 0.8, with 74% of firms reporting no change in employment levels
Prices paid index rose 6 points to 44.7 (46% of firms reported increases); prices received index rose 5 points to 21.2
Future general activity index declined 3 points to 40.0, but 56% of firms expect increases over the next six months
Median capacity utilization unchanged at 70-80%; 82% of firms reported uncertainty as a constraint, up from 62% in December
why_it_matters: This regional manufacturing survey provides an early read on factory sector conditions in the Third Federal Reserve District. The persistent elevation in price indexes (prices paid at 44.7, prices received at 21.2) signals continued input cost pressures and pricing power in manufacturing, which Fed policymakers monitor closely when assessing inflation trajectory. The future employment index jumping 26 points to 40.4 suggests firms anticipate hiring needs. The rise in uncertainty as a constraint (82% vs 62% in December) warrants monitoring.
macro_context: The survey data is broadly consistent with a manufacturing sector in expansion mode. The rise in both prices paid (44.7) and prices received (21.2) indexes suggests ongoing cost pass-through, consistent with elevated but stabilizing inflation dynamics. The strong shipments reading (22.2) contrasts with the weaker new orders index (8.6), potentially indicating order fulfillment catching up. The future employment index jumping 26 points to 40.4 suggests firms anticipate hiring needs. Energy markets are a concern, with 54% expecting worsening impact over the next three months.

issues_found: key_points[2] states '54% reported increases' for new orders, but source shows diffusion index of 8.6 (54.1% reporting increase minus 45.9% decrease). The diff index means 54.1% reported increases, which overstates the share of firms reporting increases by ~14 percentage points
key_points omits the future employment index jumping 26 points to 40.4, which is a significant signal for hiring expectations mentioned in both the article text and the summary table

human_review.notes: Minor factual issues: one percentage misstatement and an omitted but noteworthy data point. The core summary is accurate and source attribution is present.

notes: Regional manufacturing survey for the Third Federal Reserve District. Survey is not a national indicator but provides early signals for regional factory conditions. Key inflation signals: prices paid index at 44.7 and prices received at 21.2 both remain elevated above long-run averages. Uncertainty cited as a constraint by 82% of firms, up from 62% in December 2025. Source released March 19, 2026 at 8:30 a.m. ET per source body.
